title: Methods of solving the system of equations for the energy gap in the revisited BCS theory of superconductivity
author: Anghel, Preda

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Let us detail here the methods used in Ref. [3]. The starting point is the BCS Hamiltonian [4](1)H^=∑ks∈k(0)n^ks+∑klVklck↑†c−k↓†c−l↓cl↑,written in terms of the creation ck,s† and annihilation ck,s operators on the states determined by the quantum numbers (k,s) (concretely, k is the wavevector and s≡↑,↓ is the spin projection). The free particle energy is ∈k(0) and the number operator is denoted by n^ks≡ck,s†ck,s. We assume Vkl≡−V if ∈k(0),∈l(0)∈IV≡[μ−ℏωc,μ+ℏωc] and Vkl≡0 otherwise. Then, μN^ is subtracted from the Hamiltonian (N^≡∑k,scks†cks) and H^M=H^−μN^ was liniarized and diagonalized by the Bogoliubov-Valatin transformations [5,6],(2)H^M=∑k(ξk−∈k+Δbk*)+∑k∈k(γk0†γk0+γk1†γk1)where ξk≡∈k(0)−μ, ∈k≡ξk2+Δ2, Δ is the energy gap, γk0=ukck↑−vkc−k↓†, and γk1=vkck↑†+ukc−k↓ (γ† and γ are quasiparticle creation and annihilation operators, respectively). The coefficients uk and vk satisfy(3)|vk|2=1−|uk|2=12(1−ξk∈k).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: In these notations, the energy gap satisfies [4,1],(4)1=V2∑k(1−nk0−nk1∈k)

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Eq. (4) ensures the consistency of the formalism.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: In equilibrium, the populations nki are functions of temperature, as we shall see below.

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: By defining t≡ξ/Δ=±r2−1, we may write mξ and m−ξ in a single, more convenient form,(13)mt≡mξ(t)=Δt2+1(t2−at+ab+1)≡Δt2+1E(t;a,b),where E(t;a,b) is a function of t, which depends on the parameters a and b. The sign of E determines the sign of mt and the discriminant of this second order polynomial is D≡a2−4ab−4. If D>0, then there is an interval (t1,t2), with(14)t1,2=(a∓D)/2,such that, if t∈(t1,t2), then m(t) 0 and nt=0, plus nt1=nt2=1/2. The minimum value of E is Emin(a,b)≡E(tmin=a/2;a,b)=−a2/4+ab+1.

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: In the following we shall discuss only the case a>0 and the situation a 0, the solutions (20) exist and t2>0, whereas t1>0 if and only if ab+1>0 (t1<0 if ab+1<0). Using these results, we can calculate the quantities in Eq. (11):(15a)2σ0V=∫−AAdtt2+1−2∫t1t2dtt2+1=2In[A2+1+A]−2[arcsinh(t2)−arcsinh(t1)](15b)b=∫−AA(1−2nt)t(t2+1)3/2dt∫−AA(1−2nt)(t2+1)3/2dt=∫−AAtdt(t2+1)3/2−2∫t1t2tdt(t2+1)3/2∫−ℏωcΔℏωcΔdt(t2+1)3/2−2∫t1t2dt(t2+1)3/2=1t22+1−1t12+1AA2+1−t2t22+1+1t12+1≡B(A,a,b),where we introduced the notation A≡ℏωc/Δ, which, in general, is much bigger than 1, and B(A,a,b), which is the expression on the right hand side (r.h.s.) From Eq. (15a) we find the minimum value A0 of A, given by the standard zero temperature BCS equation(16)ln[A02+1+A0]=1/σ0V.

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: Furthermore, noticing that(17)arcsinh(t1,2)=ln(t1,2+t1,22+1),

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction:
Eq. (15a) may be written(18)t12+1+t1t22+1+t2=ΔΔ01+(1/A0)2+11+(1/A)2+1≈ΔΔ0(1+1+1/A02−1/A24)We introduce the notation M≡A/A2+1 and M0≡A0/A02+1. Since A≫1 in general, then M≲1. If we chose σ0V=0.2 and Δ=2×10−4 eV (the value of energy gap in an Al superconductor at zero temperature), we obtain A0≈74.2 and 1−M≤1−M0≲10−4≪1. Nevertheless, in the following we shall work with finite (but large) values of A and we will show that in general the relative differences in the physical quantities calculated with M≲1 and M=1 are of the order of 1−M. Therefore, the approximation M=1 is good enough for all the physically relevant cases. This will make (15b) an equation for b(a), which, if plugged into (15a) or (18), determines Δ(a)
[2].

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: To find the solutions of the system (15) we proceed in two steps: in Step 1 (S1) we find the solution of Eq. (15b) and, using this, in Step 2 (S2) we solve Eq. (15a).

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: S1 is further divided into smaller sub-steps. In Step 1.1 (S1.1) we take a≤2. In this case, we notice that D≥0 if and only if b≤0, so we look for negative solutions of Eq. (15b). First, we notice that there is always a solution with b=0, which corresponds to m(t)>0 and nt=0 for any t. this solution leads to the typical BCS solutions for Δ at zero temperature. Therefore, we have only to look for the second solution. For this, we notice that the numerator Bnum≡1t22+1−1t12+1 of B (Eq. 15b) satisfies Bnum≤0, since t2≥t1 (we assume that Bnum=0 and b=0 when D 0. Using the convention t2t22+1−t1t12+1≡0 when D≤0, we obtain Bden[D(a,b)≤0]=M>0. If we write t1,2 as functions of a and D (Eq. 14), then Bden(D,a,M) monotonically decreases with D for any a and M: Bden(D=0,a,M)=M and Bden(D→∞,a,M)=M−2, as one can see in Fig. 1. Therefore, the equation Bden(D,a,M)=0, as a function of D, has one solution Dinv for any M and a. From this, we obtain the value of b where the r.h.s. of Eq. (15b) changes sign, denoted as binv≡(a2−4−Dinv)/4a.Fig. 1The denominator Bden of the r.h.s. of Eq. (15b), vs. a and D, for M=1 (for realistic values of the parameters, 1−10−4≤M≤1). The function is monotonically decreasing with D, for fixed a.Fig.1:

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: In Step 1.1.1 (S1.1.1) we obtain binv numerically. Practically, this is done by looking for the solution of the equation in b, Bden(b,a,M)=0, for fixed a and M, in an interval b∈(−2/a,b0), where b0≡(a2−4)/4a is the value at which D=0. We notice that Bden(b0,a,M)=M>0 and Bden(−2/a,a,M)<0 for any a (the second inequality was checked numerically). Then, in Step 1.1.2 (S1.1.2) we find the solution of Eq. (15b) in the interval (b0,binv)

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: Step 1.2 (S1.2) corresponds to a>2. In this case, b0≡(a2−4)/4a>0, so, in principle, a solution b>0 of Eq. (15b) may exist. But, in such a case, the solution bsol should be smaller than binv [so that B(A,a,bsol)>0], which implies binv≥0. Assuming that binv=0, then(19)M=a2+a2−42(a2+a2−42)2+1−a2−a2−42(a2−a2−42)2+1≡fM(a).

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: First, we notice that(20a)fM′(a)=a+a2−4+a−a2−42a3(a2−4)>0for any a>2 and(20b)lima→∞fM(a)=1.

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: Then, we calculate the series expansion(21)fM(a)=1−1a−12a2−O(1a3)to obtain (from 21 and 19) that binv=0 if 1/a≈1−M≪1. If M=1, then 1/a=0, so there is no positive solution of Eq. (15b) for any finite a>2. If we denote by ainv the solution of Eq. (19), then, from Eq. (21) we observe that(22)1ainv+12ainv2≈1−M,which implies 1/ainv≲1−M.

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: In the general case, when M≲1, if a≥ainv, then binv≥0 and, at b=0, we obtain(23)B(A,a>ainv,0)=[1−2A2+1(1+1−4a2−1−1−4a2)−1]−1≈(1−1A2+1)−1[1+1a(A2+1−1)+1a2(A2+1−1)(32+1A2+1−1)]≳1(since a and A≫1). To calculate binv, we use the fact that a>ainv≫1 and binv≥0 Using also the assumption that binv/a≪1, we obtain the expansion(24)M=t2t22+1−t1t12+1≈1−binvbinv2+1−1binv2+11a.

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: Neglecting the term proportional to 1/a, we obtain(25)1binv2≈1(1−M)2−1 2 and therefore it does not have any positive solution for any a, at least for the values of M used in this paper, that is, 0<1−M≲10−4(≪1).

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: To prove that there are also no negative solutions of Eq. (15b) for a>2, we calculate the derivative ∂B(A,a,b)/∂a and numerically show that it is negative (see Fig. 2). Since we can also notice that b≥B(A,a=2,b) for any b 2, so Eq. (15b) has no solution for a>2.Fig. 2∂B(A,a,b)/∂a vs. a (for a>2) and xv=b/binv(a), for A=74. We observe that it is negative for all the values calculated. When xv→1, then b→binv, and both, B(A,a,b→binv) and ∂B(A,a,b→binv)/∂a diverge. The results differ insignificantly from the case A→∞ (i.e., M→1)Fig. 2:

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: Physical quantities at zero temperature and special cases Once we determine the solution b of Eq. (15b), we can proceed to Step 2 and calculate Δ directly from Eq. (18). Noticing that the relative error in the determination of Δ/Δ0 is of the order 10−4 or smaller, we can ignore the effect of finite A and A0 and calculate Δ for M=1 (i.e., A,A0→∞). We shall analyze the situation when a∈[0,2], since for a>2 there are no solutions, whereas for a 0. As mentioned above, for any a∈[0,2] there are two solutions, the first of which corresponds to b=0 and Δ=Δ0 (the typical BCS solution at zero temperature), whereas the second solution corresponds to b<0 and Δ<Δ0, as presented in Fig. 3. We observe that lima→0b(a)=−∞, such that lima→0ab(a)≡lab is finite (and negative). To calculate the limit lab, we first observe that(27)lima→0t1,2(a)=∓−ab−1Fig. 3The solution b(a) of Eq. (15b) (a) and the corresponding values of Δ/Δ0, as functions of a≡(μR−μ)/Δ (b) or (μR−μ)/Δ0. The blue, horizontal lines in all panels (at b=0 and Δ=Δ0) correspond to the typical BCS solutions, whereas the red curves represent our second solution. For these plots we used M=1.Fig. 3:

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: Since b(a) diverges when a→0 and Bnum si always finite, then Bden should become zero when a→0 and ab→lab, namely,(28)M=lima→0,ab→lab(t2t22+1−t1t12+1)=2−lab−1−labwhich, for M=1, gives [2](29)lab=−43,lima→0t1,2=∓13,lima→0Δ2=13where Δ2 is the second solution of the system (15)

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: In Fig. 4 we show the variation of t1,2 and r1,2 with the scaled asymmetry (μR−μ)/Δ0. The quantities r1,2 represent the scaled BCS quasiparticle energies corresponding to t1,2, namely(30)r2=t22+1andr1=sgn(t1)t12+1Fig. 4The interval populated by quasiparticles at zero temperature along the free single-particle energy axis [t1,t2] (a) and the BCS quasiparticle axis [r1,r2] (b) vs the scaled asymmetry of the attraction band. In (b) we also show the energy gap [−Δ,Δ] and the relative chemical potential (μR−μ)/Δ0. At μR−μ=0 we have Δ/Δ0=1/3, (Δ/Δ0)t1,2=∓1/(33), (Δ/Δ0)r1,2=sgn(t1,2)2/(33).Fig. 4

steps.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps.instruction: In (b) we also plot the energy gap (±Δ) and the relative chemical potential (μR−μ)/Δ, for exemplification.
